Basic Wire Welding Read the ENTIRE IMPORTANT SAFETY INFORMATION section at the beginning of this manual including all text under subheadings therein before welding. Safety TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY: Protective gear must be worn when using the Welder; minimum shade number 10 full face shield (or welding mask), ear protection, welding gloves, sleeves and apron, NIOSH-approved respirator, and fire resistant work clothes without pockets should be worn when welding. Light from the arc can cause permanent damage to the eyes and skin. Do not breathe arc fumes. Flux-cored wire welding is used to weld mild steel and stainless steel without shielding gas. CONTROLS MIG welding uses solid wire and shielding gas, and is used to weld mild steel and stainless steel. MIG welding can also be used to weld thinner workpieces than flux-cored welding can. Aluminum welding can be performed with an optional Sp

Duty Cycle (Duration of Use) Avoid damage to the Welder by not welding for more than the prescribed duty cycle time. The Duty Cycle Rated Duty Cycle defines the number of minutes, within a 10 minute 120 VAC period, during which a given welder can produce a 40% Use at 100 Aparticular welding current without overheating. Safety For 10 Continuous Minutes For example, a welder with a 40% duty cycle at 100 A welding current must be allowed to rest for at least 4 6 6 minutes after every 4 minutes of continuous welding. Minutes Minutes Welding Resting Failure to carefully observe duty cycle limitations can easily over-stress a welder’s power generation system contributing to premature welder failure. 100% Continuous Use at 75 A This Welder has an internal thermal protection system to help prevent this sort of over-stress. When the Rated Duty CycleWelder overheats, it automatically shuts down an

Basic Wire Welding Technique 1. Press (and hold) Trigger and contact the area to be welded with electrode wire to ignite arc. stringer bead weave bead 2. For a narrow weld, you can usually draw the wire Safety in a steady straight line. This is called a stringer bead. For a wider weld, draw the wire back and forth across the joint. This is called a weave bead and takes practice to perform properly. CONTROLS Weld MIG Gun angles, viewed from front of weld joint. 3. Direct the welding wire straight into the joint. This gives an angle of 90° (straight up and down) for butt (end to end) welds, and an Wire angle of 45° for fillet (T-shaped) welds. 90° 45° fillet weld joint butt weld joint TIG / PushPush AngleAngle DragDrag AngleAngle 0-15°0-15° 0-15°0-15° Stick 4. For MIG welding using solid wire and shielding gas, the end of the MIG Gun should be tilted so that wire is angled anywhere in-betw

Note: If Welder is used too long, a LCDwarning screen appears in the LCD ® Warning DisplayDisplay window and the unit automatically Screen shuts down. The Welder automatically returns to service after cooling off. Should this occur, rest the MIG Gunon an electrically non-conductive, Safety heat‑proof surface, such as a concrete slab, away from the ground clamp. Allow the Welder to cool with the Power Switch on, so that the internal Fan will help cool the Welder. Power SwitchWhen normal operation resumes, use shorter welding periods and longer rest periods to help prevent needless wear. CONTROLS After practice welding for a few seconds, 7. Only hold TIG Rod with an electrically insulated welding glove. TO PREVENT DEATH FROM ASPHYXIATION: Do not open gas without proper ventilation. Fix gas leaks immediately. Welding Tips A good way to test welding technique is to examine a weld’s appearance after it has cooled and the slag has been removed. Then, better welding can be learned by adjusting your weld technique to remedy any problems found. NOTICE: TIG welding is a complicated process, requiring experience and skill to achieve successful Safety results. Training beyond the scope of this manual is required to TIG weld properly. After practice welding a couple of welding beads, STOP and examine your weld using the following guidelines. Strike Test CONTROLS A test weld on a PIECE OF SCRAP can be clamp tested by using the following procedure. dead-blow hammer SCRAP WEAR ANSI-APPROVED SAFETY GOGGLES workpiece DURING THIS PROCEDURE. Wire Weld – Porosity Wire Weld – Excessive Spatter Small cavities or holes in the bead. Fine spatter is normal. Spatter that is grainy and large is a problem. TOP VIEW TOP Safety VIEW POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS 1. Incorrect polarity: POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS Check that polarity is set correctly 1. Dirty workpiece or welding wire: for type of welding. Clean workpiece down to bare metal. 2. Stick Weld – Coat of Slag Over Weld Slag is a necessary part of a stick weld. It shields the weld from impurities. Clean off the slag with the TOP Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush after welding. Problem Possible Causes Likely Solutions 1. Tripped thermal protection device. 1. If a warning screen appears in the LCD Display window, Welder may have overheated and shut down. Stop and wait with the Power Switch ON for the Welder to cool. The Welder automatically returns to service after cooling off. Reduce duration or frequency of welding periods to Safety help reduce wear on the Welder. Refer to Duty Cycle (Duration of Use) on page 19. 2. Circuit supplies insufficient input voltage or 2.
